---
title: "Patents, Papers, Parts & Planet: Mapping Local Innovation Trajectories in Synthetic Biology"
subtitle: "Master's Thesis — Complexity Science Hub Vienna"
author: "Supervised by Frank Neffke (CSH) and Claudia Doblinger (TU Munich)"
---

## Abstract

This paper examines whether iGEM student projects and academic publications in synthetic biology cluster together semantically at the city level. Using a corpus of 13,335 artifacts — 8,720 OpenAlex papers and 4,615 iGEM Registry projects — we generate SPECTER2 embeddings, project to 2D with UMAP, and compute city-level semantic overlap as the cosine similarity between the centroid embedding of a city's papers and the centroid of its projects. Across 379 cities, overlap is positively associated with city size (more papers, more projects) and with the temporal depth of iGEM engagement (years of continuous participation), explaining 63% of the variance in a robust OLS regression. As a worked case study, we examine carbon capture in synthetic biology — 375 flagged artifacts across 7 cities with sufficient data density — finding consistently high paper-project semantic overlap in known synthetic biology hubs. We discuss limitations including the absence of patent data, the cross-sectional design, and the domain mismatch between SPECTER2's training data (academic papers) and iGEM project descriptions.

---

## 1. Introduction

Synthetic biology is a young engineering discipline that treats cells as programmable systems. Using standardized genetic parts, researchers design and build novel biological functions — sensors, metabolic pathways, therapeutic circuits, and more. The field has grown rapidly since the mid-2000s, generating academic publications, commercial patents, and a distinctive artifact that is hard to classify as either: the iGEM project.

The International Genetically Engineered Machine (iGEM) competition is a global student competition in synthetic biology, founded at MIT in 2004. Competing teams design and build biological projects over the course of a year, document their methods on a public wiki, and deposit their genetic parts ("BioBricks") into an open registry. The result is a growing, well-documented dataset of student innovation that sits at the intersection of academic research and open science practice.

This paper asks a simple question: **do cities where iGEM teams are active also tend to produce academic papers in semantically related areas of synthetic biology?** More precisely, is the topical content of a city's iGEM projects similar to the topical content of its synthetic biology papers?

The motivation is twofold. First, from the perspective of regional innovation studies (Boschma, 2005; Neffke & Henning, 2013), cities tend to specialize in related knowledge domains — capabilities built in one area facilitate development in adjacent areas. If this dynamic applies to synthetic biology, we would expect cities to show coherent specialization across different artifact types (projects and papers), not random mixing. Second, iGEM is a distinctive institution: a globally integrated open-science platform that nonetheless operates through local university teams, connecting student work to local faculty, local equipment, and local academic culture. Whether this local embeddedness produces topical coherence with nearby academic output is an empirical question.

We do not claim that iGEM activity causes related academic publications, or that papers stimulate student projects. Both could reflect the same underlying factors: research infrastructure, university specialisation, faculty expertise. We use the language of *association*, *semantic relatedness*, and *co-location*.

The primary case study throughout is **carbon capture in synthetic biology** — engineering microorganisms to fix atmospheric CO₂ or produce carbon-neutral fuels. This subfield is large enough to find examples in both artifact types, directly relevant to climate policy, and allows us to test whether the city-level semantic overlap measure identifies meaningful thematic co-specialization at the subfield level.

---

## 2. Data

### 2.1 Academic Papers

Papers were retrieved from [OpenAlex](https://openalex.org), a free and open scientific literature index covering over 240 million works. We queried for papers matching core synthetic biology keywords (*synthetic biology*, *synthetic genomics*, *synthetic genome*, *BioBrick*) and subfield keywords (*synthetic gene circuit*, *synthetic cell*, *minimal genome*, *repressilator*, *xenobiology*, *cell-free synthetic biology*). This two-layer strategy follows Shapira et al. (2017), who used a similar approach to delineate the synthetic biology literature and avoid both under-inclusion (missing relevant papers that do not use "synthetic biology" explicitly) and over-inclusion (papers that use technical terms in other contexts).

The final paper corpus contains **8,720 papers** spanning 2000–2026, geocoded to **1,146 unique cities**. Papers were geocoded to the city of the lead author's institution using OpenAlex's institution metadata.

### 2.2 iGEM Projects

iGEM project data were retrieved from the [iGEM Registry API](https://parts.igem.org). Each entry records the team name, host institution, competition year, and project wiki description. Project text was formed from the team name and wiki abstract. Teams were geocoded using a three-tier pipeline: (1) OpenAlex institution lookup for recognized university names, (2) Research Organization Registry (ROR) for remaining institutions, (3) Claude Haiku for hard cases. This yielded **4,615 projects** across 626 unique cities, spanning 2009–2025.

### 2.3 Carbon-Capture Subset

Artifacts were flagged as carbon-capture relevant if their text contained at least one of the following keywords: *carbon capture*, *carbon sequestration*, *CO₂ fixation*, *carbon dioxide reduction*, *carbon neutral*, *biofuel*, *carbon cycle*, *autotrophic*, *RuBisCO*, *Calvin cycle*, *carboxylase*, *carbon concentrating mechanism*, *photorespiration*. This yielded 234 papers and 141 projects (375 total, ~2.8% of the corpus).

### 2.4 What Was Not Included

Patent data was excluded from the final analysis. An initial attempt to retrieve synthetic biology patents from Lens.org using IPC classification codes (C12N, C12P, C12Q) combined with keyword filtering produced too few records with reliable city-level geocoding to support the analysis. This is a significant limitation and a clear direction for future work.

---

## 3. Methods

### 3.1 Embeddings

All artifacts were encoded as 768-dimensional vectors using **SPECTER2** (Lo et al., 2020; Specter2, 2022), a transformer model pre-trained on scientific text using citation-informed contrastive learning. Specifically, we used the *proximity adapter* variant, which is explicitly trained for symmetric document-to-document similarity retrieval — the appropriate choice for measuring how close two documents are in semantic space.

Each document was represented by the `[CLS]` token embedding from the final hidden layer, truncated to 256 tokens. Embeddings were generated on Apple Silicon (MPS) and cached incrementally to disk. Total corpus embedding time was approximately 6 minutes.

**Limitation:** SPECTER2 was trained on scientific paper abstracts, not on iGEM project descriptions. Project descriptions are shorter, less formal, and structurally different from academic abstracts. This domain mismatch may compress the apparent distance between projects and papers compared to what a model trained on both would produce.

### 3.2 Dimensionality Reduction and Clustering

Embeddings were reduced to 30 dimensions using UMAP (McInnes et al., 2018) for clustering, and separately to 2D for visualization. Parameters: `n_neighbors=10`, `min_dist=0.05`, `metric=cosine`. Clusters were identified with HDBSCAN (Campello et al., 2013) on the 30D coordinates (`min_cluster_size=10`, `min_samples=1`), yielding 347 clusters.

The 25 largest clusters were labeled by sending the 20 titles closest to each cluster centroid to Claude Haiku (Anthropic) with a prompt requesting a 3–6 word topic label. This produced interpretable labels such as "CRISPR-Cas genome editing systems", "bacteriophage therapy and engineering", and "biofuel and fine chemical production".

### 3.3 City-Level Semantic Overlap

For each city $i$ with at least one embedded paper and one embedded project, we computed:

$$\text{semantic\_overlap}_i = \cos\left(\bar{e}^{\text{papers}}_i,\; \bar{e}^{\text{projects}}_i\right)$$

where $\bar{e}$ is the L2-normalised centroid (mean vector) of all embedded documents of that type in city $i$. Centroid cosine similarity is equivalent to the mean pairwise cosine similarity when embeddings are unit-normalised (Turney & Pantel, 2010), and is computationally efficient. The measure ranges from −1 (opposite) to 1 (identical); in practice, for a homogeneous scientific corpus, values are concentrated near 1.

### 3.4 Regression

We estimated the OLS regression:

$$\text{overlap}_i = \alpha + \beta_1 \log(n\_papers_i) + \beta_2 \log(n\_projects_i) + \beta_3 \cdot \text{year\_span}_i + \varepsilon_i$$

across three specifications adding temporal and carbon-capture controls. Count variables are log-transformed to account for right-skewed city-size distributions. Standard errors are HC3 heteroskedasticity-robust (White, 1980).

---

## 4. Results

### 4.1 Semantic Space Structure

The UMAP projection shows papers and iGEM projects interleaved across semantic space rather than clustering into two distinct regions. This is consistent with the two artifact types drawing on shared scientific vocabulary and thematic content. Carbon-capture artifacts (green diamonds in Figure 1) appear distributed across several adjacent regions — reflecting the cross-cutting nature of carbon capture as a synthetic biology application.

The 25 named thematic clusters span interpretable subfields of synthetic biology: bacteriophage engineering, CRISPR tools, biofuel production, engineered cancer therapies, minimal genome design, and DNA assembly standards. The labels are legible — a basic sanity check that the embedding-clustering pipeline is working as intended before moving to the city-level analysis.

### 4.2 City-Level Semantic Overlap

Across 379 cities with both paper and project embeddings, semantic overlap ranges from 0.80 to 0.99 (mean 0.94, SD 0.04). The distribution is left-skewed — most cities show high overlap, with a smaller tail of cities where papers and projects diverge topically.

Top cities by overlap include Cambridge (US, 0.99), Shanghai (0.99), Tianjin (0.99), Berkeley (0.99), and Boston (0.99). These are large, well-established synthetic biology research centres with focused iGEM programmes. The narrow absolute range of scores reflects the homogeneity of the corpus: all artifacts are in synthetic biology, so SPECTER2 places even diverse documents close together. Variation within this range is nonetheless real — the gap between 0.80 and 0.99 corresponds to meaningfully different degrees of topical alignment.

### 4.3 Regression Results

| Variable | (1) Size only | (2) + Temporal | (3) + Carbon |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| log(papers) | 0.0168\*\*\* (0.0015) | 0.0165\*\*\* (0.0015) | 0.0167\*\*\* (0.0015) |
| log(projects) | 0.0187\*\*\* (0.0018) | 0.0102\*\*\* (0.0030) | 0.0102\*\*\* (0.0030) |
| iGEM year span | — | 0.0016\*\*\* (0.0004) | 0.0016\*\*\* (0.0004) |
| CC paper share | — | — | 0.0188 (0.0157) |
| CC project share | — | — | −0.0086 (0.0188) |
| R² | 0.620 | 0.634 | 0.638 |
| N | 379 | 379 | 379 |

*Robust (HC3) standard errors in parentheses. \*\*\* p < 0.01.*

Cities with more papers and more projects show significantly higher semantic overlap. A doubling of papers is associated with a 0.012 increase in overlap (0.0168 × log 2 ≈ 0.012), and a doubling of projects with a 0.013 increase. These effects are modest in absolute terms — the scale is compressed because all values are near 1 — but they are precise and robust.

Temporal engagement (iGEM year span) is significantly positive: cities where iGEM teams have been active over more years show higher overlap, even controlling for total project count. A 10-year span of iGEM activity is associated with approximately 0.016 higher overlap. Cities where teams keep returning tend to be the same cities with deep, focused research programmes — the sustained engagement and the academic specialisation seem to track each other.

Carbon-capture shares are not significant in Model 3, suggesting that after controlling for city size and temporal engagement, the share of carbon-capture content does not independently predict higher overlap. This may reflect the small size of the carbon-capture subset or the breadth of the keyword filter.

---

## 5. Case Study: Carbon Capture

Seven cities have at least two carbon-capture papers and two carbon-capture projects, meeting the minimum threshold for a reliable centroid estimate. These are: San Diego (CC overlap: 0.963), Wuhan (0.959), Beijing (0.956), Shanghai (0.948), Guangzhou (0.935), Madison (0.929), and Austin (0.923). The sample is small — interpretations should be cautious — but the cities are plausible: San Diego and Madison are established biotechnology hubs; Beijing and Shanghai are the largest synthetic biology centres in China with prominent iGEM programmes.

Carbon-capture iGEM activity has grown steadily since 2015. Academic papers in the subfield were present earlier (from 2005) and show a sharper uptick post-2018 — around the same time net-zero targets became mainstream policy, though whether that connection is causal or coincidental is outside the scope of this analysis.

The carbon-capture case study cannot support causal claims: we cannot determine from this cross-sectional data whether student projects preceded or followed academic output. Both likely reflect the same city-level specialisation in bioenergy and climate biotechnology.

---

## 6. Limitations

**No patents.** The most significant limitation is the absence of patent data. The original design of this project called for a three-way comparison — projects, papers, and patents — to test a local innovation trajectory hypothesis. The patent pipeline failed due to geocoding difficulties with Lens.org data. Without patents, the "downstream translation" step of the trajectory cannot be tested.

**SPECTER2 domain mismatch.** SPECTER2 was trained on academic paper abstracts. iGEM project descriptions are shorter, less formal, and often written by undergraduates. This may inflate the apparent similarity between project and paper embeddings for short, generic project descriptions, and may understate the distance between genuinely different projects.

**Cross-sectional design.** All years are pooled. We cannot observe temporal ordering within cities. Whether cities first develop iGEM capabilities and then build academic output in the same area, or vice versa, cannot be determined from this data.

**Single-institution geocoding.** Papers with multi-institution authorships are assigned to the lead institution only. Cities that participate in large international collaborations may be underrepresented in city-specific overlap scores.

**Keyword sensitivity.** Both the paper corpus and the carbon-capture subset depend on keyword lists. Reasonable alternative choices of keywords would produce different corpora and potentially different results.

---

## 7. Conclusion

This paper provides evidence that iGEM student projects and academic papers in synthetic biology are semantically aligned at the city level, and that this alignment is strongest in cities with larger, more sustained synthetic biology footprints. The finding is consistent with the idea that cities develop coherent knowledge specialisations that manifest across different artifact types — though the analysis is descriptive and cannot identify the mechanisms driving this alignment.

The primary contribution is methodological: a reproducible pipeline for measuring cross-artifact semantic overlap at the city level using scientific text embeddings. This pipeline is generalizable to other fields where multiple artifact types (publications, projects, patents, conference proceedings) coexist. The carbon-capture case study demonstrates that the approach can identify subfield-specific co-specialisation in a small but meaningful sample of cities.

Future work should prioritize adding patent data — either through better geocoding of Lens.org records or through an alternative source such as PatentsView — to test the three-way local innovation trajectory hypothesis. A longitudinal extension, treating city-year as the unit of observation and examining whether project activity in year $t$ predicts paper activity in year $t+k$, would provide much stronger evidence for or against causal ordering.

---

## References

- Boschma, R. (2005). Proximity and innovation: A critical assessment. *Regional Studies*.
- Campello, R., Moulavi, D., & Sander, J. (2013). Density-based clustering. *ECML PKDD*.
- Cohan, A., et al. (2020). SPECTER: Document-level representation learning using citation-informed transformers. *ACL 2020*.
- Grootendorst, M. (2022). BERTopic: Neural topic modeling with a class-based TF-IDF procedure. *arXiv:2203.05794*.
- Lo, K., et al. (2020). S2ORC: The semantic scholar open research corpus. *ACL 2020*.
- McInnes, L., Healy, J., & Melville, J. (2018). UMAP: Uniform manifold approximation and projection. *arXiv:1802.03426*.
- Neffke, F., & Henning, M. (2013). Skill relatedness and firm diversification. *Strategic Management Journal*.
- Santolini, M., et al. (2023). The iGEM competition as a model system for studying scientific collaboration and knowledge dissemination. *(in prep.)*
- Shapira, P., et al. (2017). Mapping synthetic biology. *New Biotechnology*.
- Turney, P. D., & Pantel, P. (2010). From frequency to meaning: Vector space models of semantics. *JAIR*.
- White, H. (1980). A heteroskedasticity-consistent covariance matrix estimator. *Econometrica*.
- Wooldridge, J. M. (2019). *Introductory Econometrics*. Cengage.
